data.title: Tame Tariff Impact Gives The U.S. Fed Room To Cut
data.description:
By James Knightley

Decent momentum looks set to gradually fade on weaker sentiment

Second-quarter GDP growth came in stronger than expected in the US at an annualised 3.8%, and there was decent momentum at the start of the third quarter, prompting us to revise up our third-quarter GDP growth forecast from 1.5% to 2.4%. Trade and inventory data have been better than anticipated, while higher-income households in the US show little sign of slowing their spending.

Nonetheless, weakness in consumer confidence and a rapidly cooling jobs market suggest spending growth will slow. Business surveys have also come in on the softer side of expectations, with subdued investment outside of tech/AI likely to persist. There are now more unemployed Americans than there are job vacancies, and this is depressing wage growth, while five consecutive monthly drops in home prices present challenges for the residential construction sector.

US GDP growth (YoY%) and ISM business surveys advanced by 4 months

Muted tariff inflation impact allows jobs slowdown to be the focus

Another concern for US consumer spending was that tariffs would drive up prices and erode spending power. However, so far, there is little sign of this happening. The announced tariffs on individual countries and sectors suggest an applied average tariff rate of 18%, but the realised tariff rate is only around 10% based on trade and customs tax revenues for July and August. Companies appear to be willing to absorb these cost increases for now. While not good news for the fiscal deficit, this means that inflation has been better behaved.

While we assume tariff revenue will eventually rise, it means the disinflationary impulses from lower energy prices, moderating housing rent increases and weakening wage growth have more time to gain traction and help offset the eventual tariff impact on goods prices. We consequently expect a flatter, lower inflation profile than predicted just a few months ago.

As such, the Fed has acknowledged that the balance of risks to its dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment has shifted, justifying renewed interest rate cuts. Monetary policy is still viewed as being restricted, and we expect the Fed to cut rates by a further 50bp this year and 50bp next year to bring them towards neutral.

Fed to continue cutting rates towards 3%

We are currently in the middle of a government shutdown, which ordinarily would have limited long-term implications. Around three million federal government workers are not being paid, with around a quarter having been furloughed, but all workers have historically received back-pay owed to them when the shutdown ends. Government contractors don’t get compensated, though, and government work can be heavily delayed, and economic data is not released.

This time, there is a concern that there could be more negative implications, with White House officials having warned that furloughed workers involved in programmes not aligned with President Trump’s priorities could be permanently fired. Federal funding for some infrastructure projects is already being paused. As such, rather than knocking perhaps 0.1pp off annual GDP, a protracted shutdown could increase that to perhaps 0.2pp or even 0.3pp.

Looking towards 2026, lower policy rates, recent declines in Treasury yields and a weaker dollar represent a loosening of financial conditions, which is supportive of growth. Additionally, more clarity on the global trading environment may stabilise sentiment and tempt companies that have been reluctant to put money to work and hire to start spending again. But this needs to happen quite quickly, otherwise the “low hire, low fire economy” risks converting into a "no hire, let's fire" story. In such a scenario, the Fed could be forced to move policy into stimulative territory (sub-3%).

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: IonQ: Turning Bullish After Series Of Positive Events (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
IonQ (NYSE:IONQ) has been one of the more debated quantum computing names over the past year. The company provided a pure-play opportunity for investors to get exposure to an emerging quantum trend, a highly speculative, yet very intriguing part of the market.

In my last article on IONQ, I argued for a Hold position as the risk factors here remain abundant. However, my thesis has turned more bullish after a plethora of positive updates over the past month.

As discussed more in the valuation section, this stock is not for one with a weak investing stomach. IONQ remains a highly speculative asset that is betting on future revenue streams, likely multiple years away.

The drivers behind my updated bullish thesis are laid out below. IONQ has long held a unique competitive advantage in the market and their early success building out its quantum computing, networking, and security facets are starting to gain traction in the market.

I still believe that a lot of success is currently being priced into the stock. And it is likely this remains a volatile asset for the foreseeable future. If quantum is the future with a massive TAM, IONQ provides a pure-play investment opportunity to gain exposure here. Importantly, IONQ does not need to be the only market winner for the stock to keep working.

Investor sentiment is overwhelmingly bullish on quantum. Yes, there have been more headlines recently pointing out the obvious - that being these quantum stocks have sky-high valuations with minimal revenue. Valuation alone is never a great argument for a bull or bear thesis.

Bullish Updates Over Past Month

One of the biggest recent updates was the company's analyst day on September 12. The company's 114 page slide presentation is full of in-depth technological advances and why IONQ's competitive positioning is only going to improve.

The most important message was clear. The future of quantum computing will inherently be networked with quantum processors not likely to work in isolation. There will need to be quantum links in order to efficiently scale performance and effectively distribute the workloads. IONQ's networking capabilities are starting to become unparalleled in the market, making this competitive advantage even more important. By taking ownership across both the computing and networking layers, IONQ can integrate quantum memory and photonic interconnects that will ultimately improve scalability.

Although IONQ does not expect this combined stack of clustered quantum computing to be commercially viable until 2028, this approach is highly differentiated and technically challenging to replicate.

As previously laid out, IONQ has a roadmap to achieve 2 million physical qubits and 80,000 logical qubits by 2030.

This aggressive cadence of technical milestones and modular scaling, if proven to be successful, would further enhance IONQ's competitive advantage. Of course, competitors are not standing still and watching. Significant investments are being made across the industry. However, based on the data provided in the chart above, the trajectory of IONQ's qubit scaling is above and beyond what competitors have publicly talked about.

Just one week after this analyst day, IONQ signed a memorandum of understanding with the U.S. Department of Energy.

Under the agreement, IonQ will provide the design and execution of an orbital demonstration of quantum-secure communications using its satellite platform. The collaboration will also explore additional quantum applications in space, including alternate position, navigation, and timing, time synchronization, quantum networking, and sensing.

Is this an absolute needle mover? Probably not. However, it does underscore the validity behind IONQ's quantum approach, including the potential commercial application across aerospace innovation and the U.S. government's investment in space-based quantum infrastructure. It is highly unlikely that IONQ will be the only quantum player that the U.S. government relies on. In fact, the more reasonable approach would be for the government to build out a network of suppliers. Nevertheless, the recent MOU is an important milestone in terms of IONQ's technology gaining more market validity and support for commercial applications.

Probably the bigger piece of news in recent weeks was IONQ's disclosure of the #AQ 64 milestones. The IonQ Tempo system achieved a record algorithmic score qubit score of #AQ 64, three months ahead of schedule.

With each increment in #AQ value, the useful computational space for running quantum algorithms doubles. A system with #AQ 64 is capable of considering more than 18 quintillion (i.e., 2^64) different possibilities – more than 268,435,456 times more powerful than #AQ 36 (i.e., 2^36) which IonQ achieved nine months ago.

This technological achievement has only been reached by one company - IONQ. It is logical to assume other quantum technology will also reach this milestone, however, having the first mover advantage in such a nascent industry is a massive advantage.

Finally, and maybe the most underappreciated headline, IONQ appointed General John W. Raymond to the Board of Directors. For a mature, established company, Board appointments are almost a formality. Investors like to see periodic "refresh" of Board members to keep the pace of innovation high and to bring in new perspective.

In the case of IONQ - and other early market leaders - establishing a credible Board can further demonstrate market viability. General Raymond is highly recognized across space and national security operations, including having served as the first Chief of Space Operations for the U.S. Space Force. As mentioned above, IONQ's MOU with the U.S. Department of Energy was a great milestone, and establishing tighter relationships with other U.S. government agencies could help solidify IONQ's competitive advantage. Having a Board member of this quality, who has decades of government relationships, may prove to be a fruitful appointment in terms of future government projects.

Valuation

The biggest challenge with disruptive technological advancements is how to appropriately value the underlying securities. In the case of IONQ, one needs to look at the multi-year revenue potential in order to start justifying the current stock price.

The bullish investor sentiment is a difficult tailwind to face. Even if the financial statements of IONQ don't show material improvement, as long as the company is progressing with its technology and MOU, this will be viewed as positive news.

The stock now trades at essentially all-time high valuations, which are over 250x revenue. That is not justifiable. But investors are not even thinking about valuation right now.

IONQ has ~$1.6 billion in cash and equivalents, providing the company with plenty of liquidity to last for the foreseeable future. The strong balance sheet will help absorb the expected FCF losses for now, and the company's recent $1 billion equity offering shows that investors are willing to look past share dilution in favor for a strong liquidity position.

Risks

One of the biggest risks falls around the ability to commercialize this technology. IONQ has cited an an incredible $880 billion in economic value by 2040, including an $87 billion TAM by 2035. However, if the pace of commercialization takes longer than expected, or IONQ's technology ultimately is not the most efficient, the company's ability to generate future revenue would be under material pressure. The technological and execution risk here should not be understated, especially with valuation at sky-high levels.

Additionally, the end-market competitiveness will likely intensify in years to come. Larger technology companies with strong balance sheets may start to play a bigger role as this technology becomes more credible and the path to commercialization becomes clearer.

Final Thoughts

Although the stock has been on a massive bullish run over the past 18+ months, IONQ continues to solidify its competitive advantage. The analyst day and recent announcements has pushed my thesis to a more bullish tilt.

Ultimately, IONQ will not be judged on the upcoming quarter's revenue or FCF performance. In fact, the financial statement should be viewed as secondary compared to the technological advancements and MOUs the company signs. These later two factors are what will drive the stock higher, and the recent positive news flow has improved my level of confidence in IONQ turning into one of the quantum market leaders on a multi-year horizon.

For new investors to the name, it might be challenging to justify immediately building a full position. However, and this goes for existing investors, the stock will likely experience several volatile periods over the next several years. Buying on the dips can be a very fruitful approach if you are bullish on a several years time horizon.

data.title: Amazon: Don't Discount The Seemingly Forgotten #1 Hyperscaler
data.description:
Over the past three years, shares of Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) have significantly underperformed those of its rival cloud service providers (CSPs), Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOG) - see chart below. All three have been, and will continue to be, beneficiaries of the AI data center build-out, yet Microsoft and Google seem to grab the majority of the media's daily headlines despite the fact that Amazon's AWS remains the leading CSP by a wide margin. Today I'll explain why investors should not give up on Amazon and why the company has several strong tailwinds moving forward.

Investment Thesis

Amazon's AWS was the first major cloud service provider and was followed by Microsoft's Azure and still later by Google Cloud. Today, AWS continues to be the leading CSP by a wide margin:

Over the years, AWS has been the profit engine of Amazon and has enabled the company to invest heavily to grow its now global e-commerce platform as well as to make other strategic investments. The chart below summarizes the most recent calendar Q2 results of the three leading CSPs:

Q2 Revenue

YoY

Growth

QoQ

Growth

Annual

Run Rate
Amazon AWS $30.9 billion 17.5% 5.5% $123.6 billion
Microsoft Int. Cloud $29.9 billion * 26% 10.1% $119.6 billion
Google Cloud $13.6 billion 32% 11.1% $54.4 billion

*NOTE: Microsoft does not report Azure revenue, so the numbers in the chart above are for Microsoft's "Intelligent Cloud" segment, which, in addition to Azure, includes servers & other cloud services. This "accounting" gives investors the impression that Microsoft's Azure is actually closer to AWS than it really is. Indeed, Microsoft did disclose that for full-year FY2025, Azure "surpassed $75 billion in revenue," so here is a real apples-to-apples comparison of the leading CSP's TTM revenue:

TTM Revenue
Amazon AWS $116.4 billion
Microsoft Azure $75+ billion
Google Cloud $40.5 billion

As you can see, AWS is comfortably ahead of Azure and almost triple the size of Google Cloud. That said, the law of large numbers is arguably impacting AWS' growth rate, while Google Cloud is the fastest growing of the three. That's likely because not only does it have a smaller base to grow from, but also because Google's very strong position in AI (i.e., DeepMind, Gemini+ AI, autonomous driving, 9th-generation Tensor AI processors, etc.) has attracted nearly all of the emerging AI "unicorns" to use Google Cloud (see Google's Q2 conference call).

However, and while AWS is certainly a primary investment thesis in Amazon, it is far from the only driver of Amazon's stock moving forward.

E-Commerce

The graphic below (taken from Amazon's Q2 presentation) shows what an absolute and global behemoth Amazon's e-commerce platform has become:

As you can see, Amazon's AWS segment equates to only 18% of total revenue, while its North American and International segments combined for a whopping $553.7 billion of TTM revenue (82%). This is important because Amazon has been steadily increasing margin across this massive sales base. Indeed, Q2 operating income of $76.2 billion was +40% yoy, while net income of $18.2 billion was +35% yoy. Even more impressive is the fact that TTM net income of $70.6 billion was +59% yoy.

Amazon's international segment is also a very positive growth catalyst: Q2 sales were +16% yoy while operating income was +448% (those two metrics are +11% and +324%, respectively, on an F/X-adjusted basis).

Margin Expansion

Amazon is now benefiting from years of heavy cap-ex spending to grow its global infrastructure footprint. Indeed, on the Q2 conference call, Brian T. Olsavsky, Amazon Senior VP & CFO, said:

North America segment operating income was $7.5 billion, an increase of $2.5 billion year-on-year. North America operating margin was 7.5%, up 190 basis points year-over-year. International segment operating income was $1.5 billion, up $1.2 billion year-over-year. International operating margin was 4.1%, up 320 basis points year-over-year.

Margin expansion should continue due to the falling U.S. dollar (see below), the increasing use of robotics & automation, and increasing contributions from high-margin advertising. Given Amazon's massive e-commerce revenue base, even small incremental margin improvements have an outsized impact on Amazon's bottom line.

Advertising

In Q2, Amazon's advertising revenue grew 22% yoy. Revenue was driven by sponsored products and Amazon's "full funnel" advertising approach of connecting brands with customers. Advertising growth is also benefiting from Amazon Prime and the company's expansion into live sports like Thursday night NFL games, NBA, WNBA, and NASCAR. Advertising has estimated EBITDA margins of ~50%, and in FY2024, it was 9% of Amazon's total revenue. Amazon has an excellent opportunity to continue using AI to improve its advertising business' growth rate and margin.

Other Catalysts

Amazon conducts a thriving third-party business on its e-commerce platform and, on the previously referenced Q2 conference call, reported:

In Q2, worldwide third-party seller unit mix was 62%, the highest ever, up on 100 basis points from Q2 of last year.

Amazon's third-party channel equated to 24% of revenue in FY2024.

Other mid-term catalysts include potential monetization of recent acquisitions like MGM Holdings in 2022, One Medical in 2023, and EV maker Zoox in 2020-which complements Amazon's over 10% stake in Rivian (RIVN) and an agreement to deliver 100,000 Rivian vans by 2030, for which it has already rolled out 25,000.

Other bets include Project Kuiper, Amazon's low-orbit satellite broadband network, an agentic capable Alexa+, and the potential for additional pharmacy services like what Amazon Pharmacy is doing in Los Angeles. Meantime, Amazon clearly has a huge opportunity to use AI across its platform to increase and optimize sales, efficiencies, and margin.

Lastly, Amazon has invested $8 billion in Anthropic, including a $4 billion agreement announced in March of 2024 to make AWS Anthropic's primary cloud provider for mission critical workloads. In addition:

Anthropic will use AWS Trainium and Inferentia chips to build, train, and deploy its future models and has made a long-term commitment to provide AWS customers around the world with access to future generations of its foundation models on Amazon Bedrock.

Trainium & Inferentia are not only chips but are also the names of foundational models (obviously for training and inference, respectively) that run on AWS's Bedrock architecture. In addition, Anthropic's Claude models have been holding their own when compared to ChatGPT and other leading LLMs. As a result, Amazon invested an additional $4 billion into Anthropic in late 2024 to deepen the relationship. Amazon's partnership with Anthropic meaningfully reinforces AWS at this key point in the AI gold rush.

Falling U.S. Dollar

Over the short term, a falling U.S. dollar is going to be a huge boon for Amazon, given its ~$150 billion in foreign revenue from its e-commerce platform alone (i.e., that number excludes AWS's foreign revenue). That's because when its foreign revenue is repatriated into U.S. dollars, it will translate into more dollars due to the YTD ~10% drop in the value of the DXY (the U.S. dollar index). As I reported in my recent and highly viewed Seeking Alpha article, Elevated P/E Ratios: Get Used To It (And Embrace It), the collapse in the U.S. dollar is a huge tailwind for the global big-tech companies and is one big reason (among others ...) that the leading big-tech stocks have a premium valuation compared to the rest of the market.

Balance Sheet

Amazon has a very strong balance sheet: at year-end 2024, the company had amassed a whopping $101 billion in cash and marketable securities and a net-debt-to adjusted-EBITDA ratio of only 0.2x.

That said, and like all the hyperscalers, Amazon is currently spending heavily on building out its AI infrastructure. As a result, free-cash-flow generation has decreased significantly:

As you can see from the graphic, AMZN generated "only" $18.2 billion in free cash flow in Q2, down from $53.0 billion yoy (-66%). However, and much like the company's large cap-ex investments to build out its e-commerce capacity during the pandemic, AMZN can easily afford to invest heavily into AI data centers and AWS capacity to make sure it remains a leading AI hyperscaler going forward. And, just like its heavy spending during the pandemic to build out its infrastructure, it will likely take a few quarters for the benefits to show up in operating income. Meantime, it's important to understand that Amazon is not taking on debt to do so - it is funding its high-level of AI infrastructure capex from its strong cash flow generation. The bigger risk - in my opinion - is not investing enough to grow and stay highly competitive with peers Microsoft and Google going forward, both of which are also investing heavily in AI infrastructure to meet booming demand.

Valuation

Amazon currently trades at a TTM P/E of 33.7x, a slight premium as compared to the S&P 500 (31.2x) but a discount to peer Microsoft (38.3x). Google, currently my favorite big-tech stock, actually trades at a discount not only to Amazon but also to the S&P 500 (26.4x).

Given Amazon's long-term track record of revenue growth combined with its strong free-cash-flow profile (see chart above), its relatively high valuation level is rational in my opinion. The fact is, if Amazon were to significantly pull back on cap-ex for expanding its AI data center capacity, the company's FCF would pole-vault higher very quickly. In addition, Amazon ended Q2 with cash and cash equivalents of $93.2 billion - or an estimated $8.62/share based on the 10.81 billion shares outstanding at the end of Q2.

The bottom line here is that Amazon is cash-rich, and going forward, the company has excellent growth potential in AWS, e-commerce, and its other smaller business ventures, as well as superb opportunities to monetize AI across its entire platform. Amazon will demonstrate continued margin expansion due to growth in advertising, supply chain efficiencies in e-commerce, and third-party fulfillment. Earnings are likely to grow at an estimated 20% CAGR through 2027. Following an acknowledged large increase in AI infrastructure cap-ex, I expect free cash flow to recover rapidly and to potentially reach an ~$200 billion annual run rate by late 2027 (or greater than an estimated $19/share).

Risks

The global macroeconomic environment, including President Trump's trade war and ongoing shooting wars in Ukraine and Israel/Gaza, are direct threats to Amazon's e-commerce business should they worsen. So far, the company has arguably navigated the tariff challenges quite well, but this could change should inflation and slowing global economic growth negatively impact its e-commerce operations going forward.

AWS is facing capacity constraints, and Q2 margins in the segment retreated from the record high set in 1Q25. Like peers Microsoft & Google, Amazon must invest heavily to expand AWS's overall footprint, capture new customers, and secure and keep its lead as the #1 CSP.

Satellite launches for Project Kuiper will also likely pressure margins for the next couple of quarters.

Years back, Amazon decided to partner with Marvell Technology (MRVL) to design its Trainium series of AI processors. While it was smart to develop a second source and a more highly optimized and cheaper alternative to NVIDIA's (NVDA) GPUs, I wonder if Amazon may have made a mistake by choosing Marvell over Broadcom (AVGO). See Broadcom: The 'Secret Sauce' Behind Its Booming XPU Business. I say that because Broadcom is widely regarded as the global leader in high-speed networking and has over 10 years of experience working with Google on its Tensor series of AI processors. That being the case, Broadcom's XPUs are highly optimized to leverage and "play" with its leading-edge high-speed networking products. As Broadcom has said, when you have clusters of thousands (or tens of thousands...) of AI XPUs all working together, "the network is the processor."

Amazon's decision to go with Marvell instead of Broadcom somewhat reminds me of Tesla's (TSLA) decision to go "camera-only" instead of Google's Waymo choosing "camera+LiDAR" technology. The fact is, these technical platform-level decisions can have significant impacts that sometimes aren't fully apparent until years later. So, I wonder if Amazon's decision to partner with Marvell instead of Broadcom could have the same kind of negative long-term consequences for AWS similar to what Tesla is experiencing by significantly lagging Waymo in robotaxis due to what certainly appears to be a significant technical flaw in its platform.

A meaningful pullback in AI spending could negatively impact AWS's growth rate and, as a result, cause a re-rating of Amazon's valuation (i.e., P/E contraction).

Meantime, Amazon needs to continue to do the basic blocking and tackling (to use a football analogy) in its e-commerce business in order to keep earnings and margins robust and growing while it invests heavily in AI. After all, both Microsoft and Google are doing so in their non-cloud businesses. That being the case, investors should keep a sharp eye on Amazon's margins. Amazon's Q3 earnings are due out later this month: on Tuesday, October 28th.

Summary and Conclusions

Amazon appears to have turned into the overlooked red-headed stepchild of the three dominant CSPs. Yet the company continues to dominate the cloud service and e-commerce markets with its unrivaled and wide moat size-n-scale. In addition, Amazon's high-margin advertising business is growing fast and will improve profitability. That being the case, it would be foolish for investors to write off the company and underestimate the power of its AWS and e-commerce platforms going forward.

I rate Amazon a 'Buy' and suggest investors use market volatility to buy the stock on weakness ($200-$210). My 12-month target is $250/share, and by the end of 2027, Amazon stock could easily trade at $325/share based on my estimate of ~$10/share in FY27 earnings and an estimated annual run rate of ~$19/share of free cash flow. $325 a share would be +48% compared to the current AMZN price of $220 at pixel time.

The only reason I don't give AMZN a STRONG BUY is because I reserve that for Google, currently my favorite big-tech company based on its lead in AI, its fast-growing Google Cloud business, its Waymo AV operations, which are just clobbering Tesla in robotaxis, and - despite operating the #1 streaming service on the planet (YouTube) - is currently and somewhat unbelievably still trading at a discount to the S&P500.

I'll end with a 5-year total returns chart of the three leading CSPs versus the S&P 500 as represented by the VOO ETF.

As you can see from the chart, Amazon has not only drastically underperformed its CSP peers Microsoft & Google, it has also delivered well less than half the total returns of the S&P 500. I don't think this trend will continue over the next 5 years. Time will tell.

data.title: BlackLine: Visible Path To Earnings Growth Acceleration
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on BlackLine, Inc. (NASDAQ:BL) was a buy rating because I saw the potential for revenue growth to accelerate to mid-teens, and if that happens, I expected valuation multiples to go up. I reiterate my buy rating for BL for this update. My conviction is anchored on two things: (1) the new go-to-market motion that works and (2) the improved SAP partnership. At the same time, the solid traction of Studio360 and the upcoming monetization of Verity AI provide additional, high-quality growth levers. These developments give visibility for BL to achieve mid-teens revenue growth, which should drive up adjusted EPS growth and multiple expansion.

Go-to-Market Execution and SAP Partnership Are Firing on All Cylinders

One of the bullish points I noted previously was BL's pivot toward larger enterprise customers and its SAP partnership, which were the key drivers for achieving my mid-teens revenue growth expectation. Back then, my view was largely driven by the shift in strategic direction and early signs of traction. The good news is that, today, there are hard data and clear execution to further back my bullish view.

Specifically, BL’s refreshed go-to-market [GTM] motion is showing meaningful and measurable progress. At the recent investor session, management disclosed that total pipeline generation grew ~50% y/y in 1H25, which is a very impressive acceleration that, I believe, validates the new GTM model (that is working).

There are two things to pay attention to here. One is management’s comment. The new Chief Commercial Officer noted this motion as a “predictable and scalable engine,” which I take as a signal that the changes made over the past year can deliver consistent results—i.e., pipeline growth should continue. Two, the quality of deals is improving as well. Average new deal size is up 39% y/y, and the number of customers with average recurring revenue [ARR] >$1 million has increased 24% y/y. To me, these are very clear proof points that the way BL is selling, executing, and positioning itself is resonating very well with customers.

Perhaps even more important is the improvement in its SAP relationship. I previously highlighted the untapped potential of SAP’s massive on-premise customer base as a key opportunity for BL. And I was right. Management disclosed that SAP-related opportunities have skyrocketed from just 20 in February 2025 to ~170 within a few months (this is a staggering eightfold increase). This is a lot more than just customers adopting SAP; I think it really shows how effective the integration is. As co-CEO Owen Ryan put it, BL has shifted from being an “opt-in on a bill of materials” to an “opt-out,” meaning BL inclusion in SAP’s finance transformation stack is now a “default option” rather than a “discretionary option.” This is a very important shift in BL’s positioning. By becoming a built-in part of SAP’s transformation solution, BL can achieve significant scale without the associated sales cost (i.e., BL doesn’t need to hire a lot more sales reps).

All in all, I believe these developments provide strong visibility into sustained mid-teens growth, and as soon as BL starts to show acceleration (probably in Q4 since SAP mainly does its business in Q4 (per CEO Owen), which would show up in BL numbers), we should see a series of positive estimate revisions.

Studio360 and Verity AI

Another area that I paid attention to previously was the launch of Studio360. Again, we see compelling early adoption data that supports my bull case. Over 110 customers are already live on the platform, and importantly, adoption is heavily concentrated among large enterprises (~80% of these customers fall within the mega/enterprise segment). This confirms that BL’s platform vision is resonating with the highest-value customer base, the one that has the most headroom for BL to achieve ARR expansion. The ROI proof points are tangible as well. Customers using Studio360 are reporting 8–16% reductions in their average financial close cycle. Another good example is that one customer is using Studio360 to automate a workflow of ~4,500 manual tasks across 103 different entities. This helped it save hundreds of hours and accelerate their month-end close. This jump in productivity justifies a higher price point and makes the platform incredibly sticky.

These are measurable results that make the platform an easy investment case for CFOs, and as such, I am even more confident that adoption metrics here will remain very solid.

Beyond Studio360, I see Verity as the next major catalyst in BL’s growth story. Verity is what management calls a “digital workforce for finance.” Basically, it is a set of AI agents designed to take on complex, end-to-end accounting tasks that traditionally required human intervention. These AI agents are capable of executing entire workflows, such as preparing reconciliations and supporting documentation, so that human accountants can focus exclusively on exceptions and high-risk items.

What makes Verity unique and different is its trust-first architecture. Unlike typical AI systems that function as opaque “black boxes,” Verity is intentionally built as a “glass box.” It provides a clear, auditable trail for every decision it makes, allowing users and auditors to see precisely how outputs were derived. One of the key hurdles for AI adoption has always been whether the output is accurate (is the AI hallucinating?); hence, having this ability to audit directly addresses this hurdle.

In my opinion, this is a very solid product that should see strong adoption, and I don’t think there is any debate that businesses want to improve productivity. Now, what makes this even more bullish is that customers who want access to Verity must migrate to the new, higher-value platform pricing, which provides an immediate ARR uplift. From there, the model becomes consumption-based, where customers pay according to how much “work” these AI agents perform. This is a very powerful top-line growth driver, as this new product not only drives platform adoption but also ties monetization directly to the value delivered. As businesses find more value in allocating workflow to these agents, they will allocate more, and that means more revenue.

Valuation

Current consensus estimates expect BL to see ~$2.8 adjusted EPS in FY27, which is about ~28% growth vs. FY24, or slightly less than 10%/year. I believe this is too conservative given the several growth catalysts I mentioned above. Here is how I frame up the earnings algorithm: Revenue growth should go back to the low-to-mid-teens percentage (pre-FY25 levels), driven by improving SAP relationships, Studio360 seeing more adoption, Verity gaining traction, and the new GTM model capturing more pipeline growth. Assuming no margin stays flat, it implies that adjusted EPS should grow at a similar rate as well. Regarding margins, I think it is more likely it would expand as the revenue base scales (EBIT margin has been scaling in line with revenue growth), so if anything, adjusted EPS should likely grow faster than revenue.

I know comparing to SAP is not the best comparison here, but like I said before, SAP saw its forward P/E multiple expand when adjusted EPS growth accelerated. I still think this will be the case for BL. My view is that BL valuation could see a reversion to its past 2-year mean, at least, which is 26x. Multiplying that by an adjusted EPS of $3.3 (15% CAGR vs. FY24), I get to a share price target of ~$86.

Risk

The adoption of Verity AI may be slower than expected, as finance departments are inherently risk-averse, potentially delaying meaningful revenue contribution until 2027. There is also a concentration risk with the SAP partnership. The reseller agreement can be terminated by either party with just six months’ notice, which could disrupt a key growth driver if the relationship changes.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating for BL. This business is executing very well on all fronts. Its GTM efficiency improvements are translating into sales momentum, SAP is evolving into an even better growth channel, and the combination of Studio360 and Verity AI should further drive growth. Together, these factors de-risk the growth outlook and support an acceleration in both revenue and margin trajectory through FY27. As these happen, I believe BL’s multiple can re-rate back toward its historical average of ~26x forward PE.

data.title: GlobalFoundries: Positioned To Capitalize On Its China-For-China Strategy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am giving GlobalFounries Inc (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:GFS) a Buy rating for a couple of reasons. First and foremost, I believe this is a cash generating specialty foundries that has been hiding in plain sight. GFS sits where power, connectivity and reliable performance matters the most. I say this because, when I think of automotive microcontrollers, RF front-end, satellite comms and silicon photonics inside data centers, then for sure we are talking about game changing products that keep everything running as I will explain below.. The company is shipping products with growing design win momentum and a rising mix into higher margin pockets. In fact the company just reported 16% FCFs and this is an indication of strong performance as noted by John Hollister during the Q2 Earnings Call.

...adjusted free cash flow margin of over 16% in the quarter. We view this as strong performance, especially considering market conditions....

Then secondly, I believe this is the only global specialty foundry that is operating at scale. In as much as Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and TSMC (TSM) compete to make the smallest and the most advanced processors, GFS makes the practical chips that keep everything running. That is, the power management chips for automotive, RF chips for phones and connectivity chips for data centers and satellites. And clearly, these are the workhorse parts that are needed for modern electronics. And for sure, they may not be making headlines like TSM and SSNLF, but certainly customer rely on them on a daily basis. To get a better understanding of the foundry business, please refer to an article titled "Taiwan Semiconductor; The 'Go To' Factory For AI Boom" that I previously covered.

Thirdly, GFS's growth areas are now gaining real traction. For instance, the auto and comm infrastructure now make about 33% of total company's revenue. And these two segments are growing faster than the rest of the business. As an example, in 2Q 2025, automotive sales increase by 36% y/y and added 25 new car related design wins. I would say that auto chips have long product cycles and stable demand. And this makes revenues more predictable and stable.

...In automotive, we continue to outgrow the market and capture share as we expand our breadth of offerings in content per vehicle...

..A testament to this strength in the second quarter, our automotive end market grew over 36% year-over-year and comprised nearly 1/4 of total wafer revenue...

Niels Anderskouv, COO, Q2 2025 Earnings Call

So now that GFs is gaining growth traction, the company is actually turning the growth into real profits and cash. I say this because, last quarter Q2 2025, GFS earned $0.42 per share and posted the 16% FCFs. Management noted that they expect more than $1Bn in FCFs by end of 2025.

...Thanks to the team's excellent execution, we remain on track to generate over $1 billion of adjusted free cash flow in 2025...

Timothy Graham, CEO, Q2 2025 Earnings Call

GlobalFoundries is also building long-term stability through smart customer deals. For example, instead of competing on price, GFS signed multi-year agreements that guarantee a certain share of customer spending. Specifically, I note that GFS partnered with a mobile client and agreed to give GF 50% of its future chip orders. Obviously the company took a small price cut in exchange for volume and higher factory use as noted by the CEO;

...For one particular customer, we partnered to replace the fixed wafer volume component of their long-term agreement with a shift to a long-term 50% share of wallet, which is expected to result in meaningfully higher wafer revenues over the remaining life of the contract. These type of adjustments for specific customers are leading to improved utilization levels across our footprint in the second half of 2025, but will result in year-over-year ASP declines in the second half of the year for this end market and to a lesser degree, for GF overall...

Timothy Graham, CEO, Q2 2025 Earnings Call

I also believe GlobalFoundries has a global advantage, This is because many chipmakers are now rushing to spread out production across regions. And the interesting bit is that GFS already did that years ago. In fact, it has manufacturing in the US, Europe, Singapore and its now adding a ' China-for-China' partnership. This partnership is expected to allow GFS to make automotive-grade chips locally in China without risking its IP. And obviously, this gives GFS a hedge against trade tensions and tariffs.

And lastly, the company is making significant progress in newer technologies. The purchase of MIPS gives GFS RISC-V processor technology which can be used in new markets like edge AI and robotics. This is definitely a significant addition to GFS offerings. At the same time, its silicon photonics business (which are chips that move data using light) is doubling in 2025 and expected to hit $200Mn in revenue. These products are key when it comes to data centers and AI infrastructures and handling more bandwidth using less power and energy.

So, lets have a look at some of the growth drivers that I believe are key in driving GFS's stock higher.

Growth Drivers

For context, GlobalFoundries is a pure play foundry that is focused on silicon rather than bleeding edge CPUs and GPUs. And this mix is diversified across smart mobile devices, automotive, home, IoT and Comm infrastructure. And as earlier discussed, there are couple of growth drivers that I believe are key in driving GFS stock higher. First, is the China-for-China Footprint. As earlier mentioned, the agreement enables GFS's customer to access GFS production, performance and quality to serve domestic Chinese demand. In in terms of demand, China happens to be the largest markers for cars, smartphones and industrial electronics as per a report by McKinsey & Company. In fact, it accounts for almost a third of global semiconductor demand. Yet because of trade restrictions there many western chipmakers face barriers to sell or manufacture certain advanced chips in China. And this gap creates a strong need for local production. That is, manufacturing inside China for the Chinese market. And this is exactly where GlobalFoundries steps in. Because GFS will be able to service the Chinese market without violating export rules or losing control of its IP. And with the China-for-China Model, GFS is able to secure long-term supply deals with domestic automakers and suppliers who want to avoid US export risk. Even if GFS captures a small share (say 2%) of the domestic EV chip demand, that alone could translate to hundreds of millions in additional revenue over the next few years.

Secondly, the MIPS add on is a big one for GFS. And this is because GFS can offer customers not only the manufacturing process but also ready-to0use CPU designs that drop into chips for cars, robots, factories networks and intelligent edge as noted during the earnings call.

...we will expand our portfolio with acquisitions such as MIPS that bring critical capabilities to accelerate our business in the AI transition...

Timothy Graham, CEO, Q2 2025 Earnings Call

Lastly, I believe the photonics ramp is expected to grow larger. As of now the segment is tracking about $200Mn in 2025 and management expects the segment to grow larger especially with co-packaged optics which is set to begin later in the decade.

Now, lets have a look at the valuation numbers for Global Foundries.

Valuation

First, I note that the company's Long term growth FWD EPS [3-5YCAGR) stands at 24.93% while the sector median stands at 15.08%. Additionally, its FWD FCF per share growth rate is expected to be 62% while sector median stands at 15% as shown below.

Secondly, when I look at the valuation numbers, I note that the company is nearly discounted on most of the valuation multiples relative to sector median. as shown below. Specifically, I note that the FWD Non-GAAP PEG of GFS stands at 0.89x while the sector median stands at 1.83x. This suggests a 51.30% discount relative to sector median. Additionally, its FWD GAAP P/E ratio stands at 24.97x while the sector median stands at 33.75x, suggesting a 26% reduction. And lastly, the FWD EV/EBITDA is at 7.62x which is 51% lower than the sector median. This definitely suggests that GFS is discounted relative to the sector median.

In terms of peers comparison, I note that on an FWD EV/EBITDA multiple basis, GFS has a lower multiple compared to most of the peers except for United Microelectronics (UMC) which has a lower multiple of 5x. All other peers from TSMC (TSM) have their multiples around the sector median of ~15x including On semiconductor (ON) at 12x.

GFS's Key Risk

I believe that tariffs and trade policies will always remain a wild card for companies in the semiconductor space. Nevertheless, with the China-for-China strategy, I believe GFS has significantly mitigated this risk. Nevertheless, management called out inventory demand for certain consumer facing customers in Q2 which was done in anticipation of tariff impacts. This means that when things normalize, the inventory demand will definitely slow and this could eventually hurt future revenues.

...Like others in our industry, we believe that some customers took on additional inventory in the second quarter, particularly in consumer-facing markets, in anticipation of increased tariff-related impacts, which will impact demand in the second half of the year as these inventories normalize...

Timothy Graham, CEO, Q2 2025 Earnings Call

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, I believe GFS is well positioned to capitalize on the foundry business. It has an impressive strategy to capture a very restrictive market; China. GFS is not in the path to chasing headlines like TSMC and Samsung. Its a quiet, fundamentally strong company. And the valuation numbers have proven so as I have elaborated. And this is why I am giving the company a Buy rating.

data.title: Savara: Back On Track With Upcoming APAP BLA Filing
data.description:
This is my fourth Savara, Inc. (NASDAQ:SVRA) article following 07/2025's "Savara: Repairing The Molbreevi aPAP Opportunity After RTF". Savara is a 1 trick pony with its Molbreevi in treatment of Autoimmune Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis [aPAP] being that single trick.

It takes a brave, some would say foolhardy, explorer to wander the desert of clinical phase pharma land with no extra water in your canteen. Savara is doing just that with its sole reliance on Molbreevi. On that awful 05/27/2025, Monday morning when it greeted the new week with announcement of its Molbreevi RTF, the market was justifiably spooked.

After having closed at $2.84 the previous Friday, it opened at $1.92 on Monday, trading a ginormous >25 million shares on the day. While Savara shareholders bent, they never totally gave up faith.

On subsequent days' trading, Savara never dipped below that shocked $1.92 opening price; the next day it closed at $2.135 and only closed below that on a handful of subsequent occasions. With its 08/13/2025 Q2/2025 earnings release announcement of its plan to refile its BLA as discussed below, the market recovered and then some.

On 08/14/2025 it closed at $3.01 and has yet to close below that mark. In this article I report on its investment merits as it prepares its successor BLA in an effort to get Molbreevi back on track after its unfortunate RTF misadventures.

Current Plans are to refile BLA on 12/31/2025.

As noted in Repairing, Savara's RTF had to do with concerns relating to Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls [CMC]. In Savara's Q2/2025 earnings press release and business update, CEO Pauls advised that following its Type A meeting with the FDA it was:

...aligned on a path forward for the information needed to resubmit the BLA with Fujifilm as our drug substance manufacturer...with the Process Performance Qualification campaigns now complete at Fujifilm, and finalization of the analytical data package expected early in the fourth quarter, we plan to resubmit the BLA in December. (emphasis added)

In an effort to allay any concerns about recidivism on its CMC issues, he went on to note that he was:

...pleased to be working with Fujifilm, a top 10 biologics manufacturer with more than 20 approved drug substances globally and a successful inspection history with numerous regulatory authorities. (emphasis added)

Savara's 09/2025 corporate overview Presentation slide 29 below shows Q4/2025 planned BLA resubmission with Fuji as drug substance [DS] manufacturer:

This same slide posits potential approval in Q2/2026 assuming the FDA grants priority review. It also includes timeline for EU/UK MAA submission and potential approval.

Modest liquidity supports Molbreevi's next step.

In the Release Savara advised that as of 06/30/2025 it had cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of ~$146.4 million and debt of ~$29.7 million. It also characterized this liquidity as leaving it sufficiently capitalized into Q1 2027. Its presentation slide 46 stated this in more conventional terms as providing it a cash runway into Q1/2027.

Savara's latest 10-K lists its net cash used in operating activities or its cash burn as ~$89 million for 2024 up from ~$51 for 2023. Its 10-Q list its cash burn for H1 2025 as ~$53 million up from ~$43 million for H1/2024.Assuming its cash burn for H2/2025 is the same ~$53 million would have it entering 2026 with $93.4 million.

This would leave it short of the ~$110 million that would likely be its cash burn for 2026. I expect that by the time one considers expenses of a launch that its cash is more likely to take it into Q2 or Q3 2026. That said I would expect Savara to be working on a financing of some sort before an FDA decision on its Molbreevi filing.

It is in a tricky timing situation; its existing loan facility with Hercules Capital allows it a $100 million draw upon FDA approval of Molbreevi. If things are looking good in 2026 with Savara having filed its BLA as expected and the FDA having approved the filing, Savara would likely be able to negotiate an early draw to take it to FDA approval.

Conclusion

As discussed in Repairing, Molbreevi will likely be working toward blockbuster revenues if it is approved. Such a peak sales potential would justify a market cap with a 3-5X peak sales multiple. As I write on 10/07/2025 Savara's market cap is ~$0.729 billion. Molbreevi's approval would open up a potential for outsized gains.

This is a situation that demands attention from those willing to take risks in an untried clinical phase biotech. Savara's upside potential following an FDA approval is epic, it could easily triple or more. Its downside potential is for a total loss.

I consider its three largest risks to be:

  1. FDA approval;
  2. lack of marketing and promotion experience, in this regard consider the following from its 10-K: "We currently have limited marketing capabilities and no sales organization. If we are unable to establish sales and marketing capabilities on our own or through third parties, we will be unable to successfully commercialize our product candidate, if approved, or generate product revenue.";
  3. As I discussed in a previous article it has potential competitive risk — "The 10-K discusses sargramostim (Leukine), available in the U.S. for off-label use from compounding pharmacies to treat aPAP. In 04/2024 sargramostim was approved by the Japanese Pharmaceuticals and Medical Device Agency for the treatment of aPAP";

Notwithstanding these risks I am rating Savara as a "Buy". I consider its potential for gain to be more compelling than its risk of loss.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories: There May Be A Surge Coming Next Week (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT) is expected to report its Q3 earnings on Wednesday, next week. Back when the company released its previous results, FY25'Q2, on the 17th of July, ABT stock dropped almost 9%, from $131 to $120. This selloff had come, despite Abbott meeting its bottom-line expectations, and slightly beating its topline expectation.

Some analysts have asserted that the selloff had come because shareholders were expecting a large earnings beat, so the 'in-line performance' was actually a let down, despite the 14.5% climb in earnings.

Now for the upcoming Q3 results, EPS expectations are at $1.30, implying an 8.3% YoY growth rate (slower than the 14.5% brought in at Q2).

As I will elaborate on in this analysis, I believe the market is pricing in quite some fear on Abbott, resulting in historically low multiples. This, to me, seems like a clear value opportunity, given Abbott's leadership positioning on the diabetes front, and the potential to compound significantly.

As such, I am upgrading my position on ABT stock from a hold to a Buy.

Leadership in the Diabetes Care Segment

In Abbott's Q2 results, one of the main areas of strength was its Medical Devices segment, and particularly the Diabetes Care and Structural Heart areas, which together drove significant top-line growth and highlighted the company’s innovation leadership.

Diabetics Care sales reached $1.9 billion (representing nearly 18% of the total company topline), which translates to a 20% YoY jump. This, of course, reflects very strong underlying demand. Capitalizing on this strong demand, Abbott pursued a very smart integration move with Epic, a leading electronic health record [EHR] system, which allowed for direct streaming of Libre sensor data into clinicians’ workflows. This integration allows healthcare providers to access patient glucose data quite seamlessly, before, during, and after consultations, which improves care efficiency and patient outcomes.

In fact, in August, the company proved its strategic intent to build upon this playbook, when it announced a global partnership with Medtronic, in which it furthers its integration of FreeStyle Libre continuous glucose monitoring [CGM] technology with Medtronic’s automated insulin delivery [AID] and smart insulin pen systems. This also, just like the EPIC collaboration, enables real-time glucose data from Libre sensors to inform Medtronic’s insulin delivery algorithms. This helps patients maintain glucose levels within target ranges with less manual intervention.

With these initiatives, we see a clear strategy of Abbott aiming to establish a leadership position in the diabetes care ecosystem, far more than just a device manufacturer but as a platform provider that integrates monitoring, data, and insulin delivery. I believe that the company may very well push on this winning momentum on the diabetes front, which will easily help the company meet, if not, surpass its earnings expectations.

With this in mind, in the upcoming earnings session, I believe that shareholders should pay keen attention to growth metrics relating to Diabetes Care, and particularly any commentary on Libre adoption, integration progress with Medtronic, and the impact of these partnerships on patient engagement and retention.

Another important area to keep watch of would be updates on revenue contribution from the newly integrated systems, and guidance on how these collaborations are expected to influence top-line growth and margins in the segment, looking forward.

Valuation Assessment

I last covered Abbott Labs in October 2024, which is almost exactly a year ago. Back then, while acknowledging the company's strong moat, I argued that the stock was fairly valued at its then price of $117, and thus had given it a hold rating. In fact, ABT's 15% price change is almost exactly the same as the S&P 500's 16% climb, which, I feel, validates my hold rating on the stock.

This time around, at its price of ~$134, the stock's trailing PE [GAAP] ratio of 16.8x seems a lot more attractive than it has been in years:

Not only is its current PE ratio of 16.8x almost half the stock's 5-year average, but it actually reflects quite the discount to the health care sector's median PE ratio of ~27x. This, to me, seems like a strong value opportunity, especially when we observe that Abbott is expected to see its EPS climb by 12.4% over the next 12 months, which is not very far off from the sector median growth expectation of 14.3%. This minute discrepency does not justify, in my opinion, the stock trading at a PE ratio that is 38% below the sector median.

The value opportunity becomes even more clear once we consider Abbott's strides in attaining a leadership position on the diabetes front, which I believe is increasingly shaping up to be one of the most defensible and fastest-growing verticals in medtech.

As such, especially with the compounding potential inherent to the company (arising out of Abbott's positioning at the intersection of healthcare and data), I am upgrading my position on ABT from a hold to a buy. The stock seems quite valuable at its current price with a PE ratio of only 16.8. Moreover, I also believe that the company may bring in a strong earnings beat this quarter, on account of a low expectation set (which may explain the cheap multiples).

Risk to Thesis

Reading through this analysis, it would seem pretty clear that Abbott’s supposed leadership in Diabetes Care and Medical Devices is quite central to my bullish outlook of the company. I should highlight, however, that these are some very high competition battlegrounds. The space is dominated by a few giant players such as Medtronic, Johnson & Johnson, Siemens, Phillips, etc.

In fact, these players are not only well-capitalized but are actually quite aggressively innovating across the same categories Abbott is betting on (continuous glucose monitoring, cardiac rhythm management, structural heart solutions, etc). In such an environment with rapid technological advancement, product differentiation can often be short-lived, and competitors can quickly match or even outpace Abbott’s innovations. If this were to happen (and it certainly isn't impossible), my thesis would fall apart, as the stock would not actually be offering much value at its current prices.

In essence, Abbott's growth in these high-value areas is far from guaranteed, and investors should consider the competitive risks before making a buy decision, based on my recommendation. The fact that there has been some heavy insider selling in the last 2 months, does not show a lot of confidence from the company's management:

That being said, I feel very strongly that the stock's low multiples give it a margin of safety, as its PE ratio implies that expectations are already quite muted. If Abbott, despite the competitive pressures, continues to execute well in Diabetes Care and Medical Devices (for instance, by expanding Libre adoption, successfully integrating with partners like Epic and Medtronic, etc.), it could actually outperform these conservative market expectations.

Investor Takeaway

When the market undervalues the potential of a company as strong as Abbott Labs, especially when it is strategizing to embed its offerings in critical ecosystems to ensure compounding growth, there is a very clear value opportunity. I believe this to be the case with Abbott. The stock offers sustainable, long-term growth, and as such, I am setting a buy rating on it.

data.title: Bank On Soaring LNG Demand With 11% Yield: Kimbell Royalty Partners
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Kimbell Royalty Partners, LP (NYSE:KRP) is one of the largest owners of pure mineral and royalty interests across the leading energy basins in the United States. KRP maintains mineral ownership in more than 17 million gross acres with more than 131,000 wells. Most importantly, as a royalty interest owner, KRP doesn't have any operating costs or capital expenditures associated with its acreage. They simply collect a portion of the revenues generated by exploration and production firms that drill in their acreage, determined by their royalty rate, the volume extracted, and the commodity prices.

Note: KRP is a C-Corp for tax purposes and issues a 1099 to shareholders.

Strategically Sound Asset Base

KRP's footprint is extensive. Approximately 98% of all onshore rigs in the lower 48 states are located in counties where KRP holds mineral interest positions. As of Q2 2025, 60% of all active rigs within KRP’s acreage were in the Permian Basin. Source

Expanding Market Share

According to the Baker Hughes Rig Count, there were 573 active rigs in the U.S. as of December 2024. At that time, KRP reported 87 active rigs. Baker Hughes data showed 537 active rigs as of June 30, 2025, while KRP had 88. This shows that while the overall rig count in the country declined by 6% in the first six months of 2025, KRP has one more operational rig, demonstrating the strategic positioning of its assets. Looking at the rig count and market share over time, we see a relatively stable rig count (barring the anomaly created by the COVID-19 oil crash), with KRP growing its market share since its IPO in 2017. Source

KRP is a company that has grown through acquisitions. Since its IPO, the company has completed more than $2 billion in M&A transactions and grown run-rate average daily production by more than 8x. Even though the company’s active rig count has dropped since the peak in 2023, its run-rate average daily production continues to be near all-time-high levels. Source

Diversified and Creditworthy Customer Base

At the beginning of 2025, KRP reported 1,400 operators actively producing on its acreage, with their top 10 operators accounting for ~41.2% of total revenues (Data Source: 10-K). The diagram below shows the top producers in each region as of Q2, representing some of the biggest E&P names in America. Source

The company notes in its annual filing that due to the high number of producers actively producing on its acreage, the loss of a single operator will not have a material effect on the financials. During FY 2024, 29% of the top line was associated with natural gas and NGL production, while oil sales generated 71%. Mineral interest owners are typically entitled to ~15-25% of production revenues, and these claims are considered senior to all other debt on the capital structure of E&P firms.

For the second quarter, 67% of the production mix was NGL and natural gas. While oil prices significantly influence KRP’s revenues, locations like Haynesville, with 94% natural gas production, provide resilience through down-cycles in oil prices.

Variable Yet Steady Dividend Stream

Since 2017, KRP has returned 71% of its IPO price via quarterly cash distributions. While this is an impressive feat, there should be no doubt about the fact that KRP is a variable dividend payer. As we discussed earlier, KRP’s revenues are determined by its royalty rate, the volume extracted, and commodity prices. Commodity prices and production volumes are inherently volatile, and this affects the company’s revenues and, in turn, its DCF (distributable cash flows). We can see the fluctuation in distribution shows a certain degree of variability over time, and barring anomalies like the global pandemic, it has been rather steady.

Note: 93% of KRP's distributions in 2024 constituted return of capital (tax-deferred reductions to the tax basis of each recipient’s ownership interest). The company noted in its press release that 100% of the Q2 dividend would constitute ROC for tax purposes. This makes KRP a good fit in retirement and non-retirement accounts.

Estimating 2H 2025 Dividends

The good news is that KRP’s future dividends can be estimated to a certain extent since the company maintains a target to pay out 75% of its DCF to shareholders and provides forward-looking guidance on production volume, barrels of oil equivalent per day (Boe/D).

At the end of Q2, management affirmed their production guidance from the beginning of the year, which was 25,500 Boe/d for the year (at the midpoint) and a range of 24,000-27,000 Boe/d. Our model uses estimates of commodity prices, production volumes, and projected expenses to estimate a $0.36/share quarterly payout. The shrinkage of KRP’s preferred dividend expense following the redemption of 50% of the units outstanding removes a significant headwind from the bottom line. However, the company has seen its share count rise by ~13 million shares in the first six months of the fiscal year. Mineral royalty companies grow through acquisition, often completely supported by equity offerings. The key is to grow acreage and run rate production enough to compensate shareholders; i.e., make a bigger pizza while inviting more people to the party. Commodity prices are an uncontrollable variable.

Annualized, our estimated 2H 2025 $0.72/share dividend calculates to a 10.6% annualized yield.

KRP insiders maintain significant ownership, with ~10% of the company shares owned by management, the board, and affiliates, and this ensures strong alignment of interests.

Conclusion

KRP is a well-managed company that provides access to hydrocarbon royalty interest, with a heavier allocation to natural gas and NGL production. U.S. LNG exports are seeing high demand in overseas markets, particularly Europe, as they look to phase out Russian energy exports. Moreover, data centers are being built with natural gas as the fuel of choice (currently, natural gas powers 40% of the data center load). These export volumes and data center demand will have multi-year contracts (measured in decades). As such, we expect steady production volumes ahead, and KRP’s proven ability to grow its acreage in strategic locations and increase its run-rate production provides defense against volatile commodity prices.

Amidst weaker oil prices, we find KRP to be an opportunity to lock in a steady high yield, with the possibility of benefiting from elevated prices in the future. Even otherwise, current run rates, asset profile, and the high-margin business model create strong prospects for reliable income from this industry leader.

data.title: This Time Really Is Different - The Market Shift No Investor Can Ignore
data.description:
Introduction

As someone who has been focused on business cycles for more than 12 years, I have to say that this is an incredibly "weird" economic environment, as unprofessional as that may sound.

For starters, this is what consumer sentiment looks like:

Consumer sentiment is now at Great Financial Crisis levels after the entire post-pandemic recovery has been erased. That's bad news, as private consumption accounts for more than 60% of U.S. GDP. The peak was 68.8% in December of last year.

To make matters worse, hiring has completely imploded, according to ADP Research:

In this case, it obviously doesn't help that an increasing number of professionals (many of whom I have covered this year) are coming out to explain just how replaceable everyone has become. This includes comments from the Walmart CEO (who employs more than 2 million people):

"It's very clear that AI is going to change literally every job," McMillon told The Wall Street Journal in an interview that published on Friday, adding: "Maybe there's a job in the world that AI won't change, but I haven't thought of it." - Via CNBC

Meanwhile, the ISM Services PMI has fallen below 50 (that's the neutral line) for the first time since May 2020:

Meanwhile, the ISM Manufacturing Index has reported its seventh straight month in contraction territory, with no attempts at growth since the second half of 2022. This marks one of the longest periods without growth in the history of this index.

And to make matters worse, inflation is still a problem, with growth of consumer prices close to 3.0% and consistently elevated 1-year forward inflation expectations, as we can see below (it's one of the reasons why I call for a "higher for longer" environment):

Yet despite all of this, this is what the stock market's valuation looks like:

Based on all of this, I think it's fair to say this is a truly tricky environment.

However, it's also one of the most unique environments. I know this sentence doesn't make sense, grammatically speaking, but I think you know what I mean.

This isn't a straightforward situation where we can say something like "Macro looks horrible, which means high valuations indicate we should sell all of our shares."

If the situation were a bit different, that could certainly be the case, but it isn't. Here's what Apollo Global Management (APO) wrote with regard to the macro environment:

1) Trade policy uncertainty is improving

2) Economic policy uncertainty is improving

3) Consumer expectations to business conditions are improving

4) Consumers are less worried about losing their jobs

5) Corporate capex plans are improving, and jobless claims are still low

6) The daily TSA data for the number of people traveling on airplanes is strong

7) Data for the number of people going to Broadway shows, the movies and visiting the Statue of Liberty is strong

8) Weekly data for same-store retail sales is strong

9) Weekly data for bank lending is accelerating

10) Weekly bankruptcy filings are starting to trend lower

11) Weekly data for business formation is still strong

In other words, the situation is weird, which creates challenges and opportunities.

Hence, in light of countless articles on why it's different this time, I decided to take a different direction in this article, as I think we're headed into a market shift that could allow us to generate substantial alpha for many years to come.

So, let's dive into what I expect will become a very in-depth market discussion!

Big Tech Dominance is Running Into Trouble

One of our most discussed topics is the top-heavy S&P 500.

These are the biggest holdings of the S&P 500:

NVIDIA (NVDA) alone accounts for nearly 8.0% of the entire S&P 500. The biggest ten holdings have a weighting that now exceeds 40.0%, the biggest number in decades.

This concentration is obviously a big reason why the market enjoys so much trust from investors (read: a high valuation). The Big Tech companies have been hitting it out of the park for more than a decade, enjoying increasingly wide business moats, super healthy balance sheets that turned into cash machines when rates rose, and massive secular tailwinds like booming cloud computing, software, personal networking, and now AI.

However, now, there seems to be a shift.

Below is a quote from a portfolio manager who was quoted by the Bear Traps Report on September 30:

The mo***s are all buying NVDA at $4.6T mkcap 29x sales, just own NatGas plays: the best risk reward AI play on the board ex-coal." - Bear Traps Report (paywalled newsletter)

While one could make the case that it's not the most professional quote, I think it touches on two very important points:

  • Nvidia (like most of its Big Tech peers) isn't cheap.
  • The Big Tech companies have become reliant on AI, a development that has consequences.

Another reason I brought up this quote is that it's getting support. The transcript below is from Rihard Jarc, who posted it on the X platform earlier this month, noting that an Alphabet employee made clear that it's not about GPUs and TPUs but about power.

To make matters worse, the Big Tech companies are increasingly competing with each other.

See, as most of the Big Tech companies are spending hundreds of billions on CapEx to make sure they aren't left behind, they are increasingly fishing in the same pond. In the past ten years, that wasn't the case, as most brought something unique to the table, except for cloud computing (AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, etc.).

For example, OpenAI is currently valued at roughly $500 billion, according to its latest funding rounds that included the sale of shares worth $6.6 billion. While this needs to be taken with a grain of salt, it is a fantastic example of Big Tech disruption due to its size, funding capabilities, and platform that can easily be scaled.

  • OpenAI is now integrating checkout systems, which means it paves the road for in-app purchases. So, if you ask ChatGPT what the best shoes are for less than $400 (or whatever), it can show direct purchasing options. This directly competes with Google and other ad-based platforms.
  • OpenAI Sora 2 is a video platform that generates its own videos using AI. I brought it up in an article a few days ago, as it also shows the crazy advances of AI, as some videos seem so real that they're almost indistinguishable from fake. This platform directly competes with Instagram (owned by Meta (META)) and TikTok.

Don't get me wrong. I am not saying that OpenAI will successfully disrupt its competitors. All I am saying is that these are new risks that simply did not exist before AI became a thing.

So again, that's one of the big reasons why Big Tech is throwing capital at AI as if there's no tomorrow, as we can see below:

According to Bloomberg, by 2030, AI companies will likely need close to $2 trillion in combined annual revenue to fund the computing power they need to deal with (expected) demand. Their revenue could come in $800 billion below that target.

This quote sums it up quite nicely:

Yet never before has so much money been spent so rapidly on a technology that, for all its potential, remains somewhat unproven as a profit-making business model. Tech industry executives who privately doubt the most effusive assessments of AI's revolutionary potential - or at least struggle to see how to monetize it - may feel they have little choice but to keep pace with their rivals' investments or risk being out-scaled and sidelined in the future AI marketplace. - Bloomberg

So far, this has created a divergence, where the Mag-7 are being outperformed by basically all major AI data center suppliers and beneficiaries (see below). That's a big deal(!).

So, what does this mean for the S&P 500?

It's Time to Diversify & Where I'm Looking for Value

For the Standard & Poor's 500, this means the handful of companies that have driven the market are now starting to somewhat lose their dominant positions. Essentially, and please correct me if you disagree, Big Tech is no longer the "can't miss" trade, which makes the index itself much less predictable.

This does not mean the market is in for weakness, but it does mean the old playbook may no longer be working. Or, to put it differently (and this is key), broad market exposure may no longer be the best way to invest. That's one of the reasons why I believe it's time for a rotation, as the stock market is now increasingly obvious a "market of stocks."

Essentially, the market's biggest players are all throwing hundreds of billions at AI to see what sticks. If something sticks, they can massively expand their market shares in the biggest disruption since the Industrial Revolution. If they miss, it could cause them to lose market share and eventually become unloved by investors.

However, that sounds way scarier than it is.

For starters, I would limit one's dependence on these Big Tech stocks. Personally, I think Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Tesla (TSLA) will all be winners, as Amazon adopts AI for its massive installed base, Alphabet is a leader in AI models, and Tesla has turned into a power giant with the best platform for humanoids, something we will see a lot more of in the years ahead.

So, no, I don't dislike all Big Tech stocks.

What I do believe is that a few things will happen.

Unlike the past decade, the next decade will not be led by these Mag-7, FANG+, or whatever you want to call this group of stocks. They are increasingly competing with each other in an environment where literally (yes, really) all of their products and services are prone to disruption. Think of cloud computing, personal computing, social media/networking, mobility, personal electronics, and more.

This benefits bottlenecks. All of these billions have figuratively hit a wall, as Google has made clear. There is simply no power to fuel AI innovation. On paper, AI innovation is much stronger. In real life, it's now entirely blocked by shortages in places like power generation.

For example, three players dominate roughly 70% of the entire global gas turbine market.

Demand for these turbines is so high that 2025 is expected to be the first year when production capacity is not sufficient to keep up with demand since 2011. This is expected to worsen and last for at least six years, which is how far the outlook below goes.

This makes power way more expensive. According to Bloomberg, which used data from NextEra Energy (NEE), a combined cycle-gas plant cost roughly $800 a kilowatt in 2021. Now, that number could be close to $2,800.

As a result, companies like GE Vernova (GEV) have blown the S&P 500 out of the water when it comes to their stock price performances since April 2024, as we see below.

GE Vernova now trades at a blended P/E ratio of roughly 100.0, with 60% and 50% EPS growth expectations in 2026 and 2027 (FactSet), respectively. That's the result of pricing power and products that sell themselves. It's like selling water in the desert.

However, while I am bullish on GE Vernova and its peers, I think the trillions in AI spending we will see in the future will trickle down a lot. After all, there is a limit to turbine production. It literally cannot go any faster. The bottlenecks are there, and capital has to go somewhere.

This alone is why I expect a few sectors to outperform the S&P 500.

  • While I dislike utilities as an investment (see this article) due to their limited pricing power, I believe the environment has shifted for these companies. The utilities (XLU) versus the S&P 500 total return (including dividends) ratio bottomed in 2024. I think this uptrend lasts.

  • I could be wrong, but there's no doubt in my mind that energy stocks (XLE) will beat the market going forward. As I noted in this article, natural gas may be the best way to play AI for many. Even if regulated utilities are overwhelmed with demand, demand could go behind the meter, as data centers are being built on top of pipelines. I think the energy/SPY ratio bottomed after the pandemic. After a downtrend in recent years, I think the risk/reward is as good as it gets. This includes landowners, pipeline companies, and oil/gas producers, among related support companies.

  • Industrial companies (XLI) are in a terrific spot, as they produce the goods needed for data centers, grid modernization, pipelines, and (even better), they could benefit from accelerating economic re-shoring in the years ahead.
